meta.title: Pattern Studio — document mining
meta.note: No live LLM in this build: candidate selection is deterministic and evidence-gated. fewShotPrompt is the exact prompt a real extractor would receive — wire it in at PIQ.patternStudio.mine().

stages.label: Ingest & Classify
stages.blurb: Parse each document; pull tables, reason codes, amounts, dates and freetext.

stages.label: Signal extraction
stages.blurb: Find what recurs across the corpus: reason codes, timings, triggers, entities.

stages.label: Pattern hypothesis
stages.blurb: Few-shot against the existing library — propose patterns it does not yet hold.

stages.label: SME review
stages.blurb: You are the gate. Accept, edit, merge or reject each candidate.

stages.label: Calibration
stages.blurb: Run the accepted detector against the uploaded transactions to set a threshold.

uploadCategories.label: Transactional Evidence
uploadCategories.blurb: The richest seam — what actually happened, transaction by transaction.

slots.label: Exception logs
slots.hint: Blocked invoices, failed postings, held orders, credit check failures

slots.label: Aging reports with notes
slots.hint: Collector freetext fields — the real story

slots.label: Payment history / remittance
slots.hint: Timing patterns, partial payments, broken promises

slots.label: Credit memos & write-offs
slots.hint: What was given up on, and why

uploadCategories.label: Process & Policy
uploadCategories.blurb: What the organisation says it does.

slots.label: Process documentation
slots.hint: SOPs, work instructions, L3/L4 process maps

slots.label: Credit / collections policy
slots.hint: Limits, review triggers, approval matrices, dunning playbooks

slots.label: Escalation protocols
slots.hint: When and how issues move up the chain

uploadCategories.label: Operational Analytics
uploadCategories.blurb: What the numbers already told you.

slots.label: Process mining exports
slots.hint: Celonis, Signavio — event logs, variant analysis

slots.label: Cycle time / touch-count reports
slots.hint: How long, and how many humans, per transaction

slots.label: Root cause analysis
slots.hint: Past improvement projects — what they found

uploadCategories.label: Organizational & Behavioral
uploadCategories.blurb: Who does the work, and what they're rewarded for.

slots.label: Skills matrix / capabilities
slots.hint: What the team can and can't do today

slots.label: Training materials
slots.hint: What staff are taught to look for

slots.label: Dunning history
slots.hint: Letter templates, send dates, response rates

uploadCategories.label: External & Contextual
uploadCategories.blurb: The world the process operates in.

candidates.name: Seasonal Treasury Squeeze
candidates.mentalModel: They pay us fine for ten months, then quarter-end lands and suddenly everyone is 'awaiting approval'. It is not distress — it is their treasury calendar, and we are the float.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: Payment delay clusters in the last 10 days of the customer's fiscal quarter, then normalises. Correlate BSID clearing dates against the customer's fiscal calendar.
candidates.layer2_eventSeries: Invoice_Posted → Due_Date_Passed → Payment_Delayed (quarter-end window) → Payment_Received (quarter+1 week)
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT kunnr, AVG(CASE WHEN quarter_end_window THEN days_late END) - AVG(CASE WHEN NOT quarter_end_window THEN days_late END) AS skew FROM ar_clearing GROUP BY kunnr HAVING skew > 9

branchingDAG.condition: IF Skew rising QoQ for 3 quarters

evidence.excerpt: "Customer AP confirms invoice approved — payment run moved to next quarter per their treasury policy." (recurs 34× across Q1–Q3 notes)
142 held/late events cluster in the final 10 days of Mar / Jun / Sep — 3.1× the base rate.
Days-to-pay rises from 41 to 58 in quarter-end weeks, then reverts.

candidates.name: Remittance Format Churn
candidates.mentalModel: Every month the remittance file looks different. Not corrupt — different. Enough to break auto-match and buy them a week of float while we key it by hand.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: Remittance advice layout/field-order changes month over month for the same payer, driving auto-match failure without any change in payment volume.
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT payer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT remit_schema_hash) AS schema_variants, SUM(automatch_fail) AS fails FROM cash_app_lines GROUP BY payer_id HAVING schema_variants >= 3

branchingDAG.condition: IF Churn persists 3 months AND Unapplied > $250K

evidence.excerpt: 4 payers account for 61% of auto-match failures; each shows ≥3 distinct remittance layouts in 6 months.
214 unapplied cash lines, 88% traced to remittance parse failure rather than payment shortfall.

candidates.name: Approval Threshold Shadowing
candidates.mentalModel: Nobody breaks the approval rule. They just never quite reach it. Every credit memo lands a few hundred dollars under the line that would have sent it to me.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: Credit memo / write-off amounts cluster immediately below a documented approval threshold, indicating deliberate sizing to stay within delegated authority.
candidates.layer2_eventSeries: Credit_Memo_Raised → Amount_Just_Below_Threshold → Auto_Approved → Posted (no second signature)
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT approver, COUNT(*) FROM credit_memos WHERE amount BETWEEN threshold*0.90 AND threshold*0.999 GROUP BY approver HAVING COUNT(*) > 10

branchingDAG.condition: IF Amount within 10% below threshold AND Approver_Repeat_Count > 5
IF Cluster spans multiple approvers on one customer

evidence.excerpt: "Credit memos above $25,000 require Controller counter-signature." (Write-off policy v4.2, §3.1)
Internal audit FY24-07: "a material share of memos fall in the $22.5K–$25K band; management response pending."
2 credit analysts hold both memo-raise and memo-approve rights.

candidates.name: Escalation Dead-Zone
candidates.mentalModel: The playbook says escalate at day 45. The collectors escalate at day 70. That 25-day gap is where the money goes quiet — and nobody owns it.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: A structural gap between the escalation protocol's documented trigger and the point at which escalation is actually observed in the communication trail.
candidates.layer2_eventSeries: Dunning_Sent → No_Response → (policy trigger passes, no action) → Escalation_Raised (late)
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT AVG(actual_escalation_day - policy_escalation_day) AS lag FROM dunning_events JOIN escalation_policy USING (segment) HAVING lag > 14

evidence.excerpt: "Accounts 45 days past due are escalated to the Collections Manager." (Escalation protocol, §2)
Median first manager-level contact occurs at day 70; 210 accounts sat 45–70 days with no logged action.
Dunning letter 3 of 3 sent, then a median 24-day silence before any further activity.

candidates.name: Incentive-Driven Month-End Discounting
candidates.mentalModel: The credit memos spike in the last three days of the month — the same three days the sales scorecard closes. That is not a coincidence, it is a comp plan.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: Concessions and credit memos concentrate in the final days of the incentive period, correlating with a scorecard metric rather than with any customer event.
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT DAY(posting_date) AS d, SUM(memo_amount) FROM credit_memos GROUP BY d HAVING SUM(memo_amount) > 3 * (SELECT AVG(daily_memo) FROM ...) AND d >= days_in_month - 2

branchingDAG.condition: IF Spike > 3× baseline in final 3 days
IF Same rep repeats across 3 periods

evidence.excerpt: Sales scorecard closes on the final calendar day; "net revenue booked" carries a 40% weighting.
38% of credit-memo value posts in the last 3 days of the month, against 10% expected.
Rep onboarding deck: "use goodwill credits to protect the close."

candidates.name: Contractual SLA Weaponisation
candidates.mentalModel: They read the contract more carefully than we did. Every late delivery, however trivial, becomes a documented SLA breach — and a licence to hold the whole invoice.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: Customer cites contractual SLA/penalty clauses to justify withholding, at a rate far exceeding the actual incidence of service failures.
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(sla_claims) / NULLIF(COUNT(actual_breaches), 0) AS ratio FROM disputes GROUP BY customer_id HAVING ratio > 3

evidence.excerpt: MSA §9.4: "Customer may withhold disputed amounts pending resolution of any service level failure."
17 threads cite §9.4 verbatim; only 4 correspond to a logged delivery exception.
SLA-coded disputes: 41 raised, 11 substantiated on investigation.

candidates.name: Onboarding Data Debt
candidates.mentalModel: Half our exposure sits behind customer records that were opened in a hurry and never finished. No risk rating, no terms, no bureau pull — and no limit that means anything.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: Customer master records created without complete credit attributes correlate with materially higher downstream delinquency.
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT completeness_bucket, AVG(days_late) FROM customer_master JOIN ar_aging USING (kunnr) GROUP BY completeness_bucket

evidence.excerpt: 31% of active customers have no risk rating; 18% carry the system-default payment term.
Incomplete-record customers are 2.4× more likely to breach 60 days.
No bureau file on record for 22 of the top-100 exposures.

candidates.name: Reopen-and-Restart Dispute Loop
candidates.mentalModel: The dispute is closed. Then they reopen it on a new reason code, and the clock starts again from zero. Same invoice, third reason, ninety days gone.
candidates.layer1_logicalMapping: The same invoice is disputed repeatedly under successive reason codes, each reopening resetting the resolution SLA clock.
candidates.layer2_eventSeries: Dispute_Closed → Reopened (new reason code) → SLA_Clock_Reset → Repeat
candidates.conceptualSQL: SELECT invoice_id, COUNT(DISTINCT reason_code) AS codes, COUNT(*) AS reopens FROM disputes GROUP BY invoice_id HAVING reopens >= 3

evidence.excerpt: 61 invoices carry ≥3 dispute records, each under a different reason code; mean age 118 days.
"Closing this one out — customer has come back on pricing instead of delivery." (collector, 9 similar threads)

fewShotPrompt:
You are a process transformation expert analysing discovery documents to identify
behavioural patterns in {function} processes.

Here are examples of known patterns from the existing library:
{examples}

Now analyse the following documents and signals:
{signals}

Identify NEW behavioural patterns not already captured in the library above.
For each candidate pattern, provide:
 1. name          — a concise descriptive name
 2. featureSlug   — kebab-case identifier
 3. description   — what this pattern represents and why it matters
 4. triggers      — observable conditions that indicate this pattern
 5. originalDAG   — the happy-path action sequence (straight-through resolution)
 6. branchingDAG  — variation branches with tiers (primary/escalation/immediate),
                    actions, and HITL gates
 7. evidence      — which documents and signals support this pattern
 8. confidence    — 0-1 score for how strongly the evidence supports this
 9. similarTo     — if this resembles an existing pattern, which one and how

Structure each candidate pattern EXACTLY like the examples above.
